Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. With 12 courses to juggle, I mostly just bounced around between whatever seemed most urgent. I learn better by doing things hands-on anyway, so sitting down to read or watch lectures felt like a chore. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd have stretches where I wouldn't touch certain courses for days, then suddenly cram when I realized something was due. Life stuff kept getting in the way too—work, family things, just feeling too tired to care.
Q3: Usually it was because I had an assignment coming up or I needed to check if I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would push me to log in. Occasionally I'd go on just to see if there was anything new, but that was rare. It wasn't something I looked forward to, more like something I had to do.
Q4: I guess toward the middle of the semester, logging in became more automatic because I was watching videos pretty regularly. The peer support group I was connected with would remind me about stuff, so that helped. But it never really felt natural—more like a habit I forced myself into because I knew I needed to pass.
Q5: The videos were actually helpful because I could pause and replay parts when I didn't get something. I watched a lot of them, way more than I attended actual classes. Discussion boards were okay too because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Anything where I could interact or see practical examples worked better for how I learn.
Q6: Reading materials and long PDFs—I barely touched those. They just sat there in my downloads folder. I learn by doing, not by reading walls of text. Optional quizzes and extra resources got ignored too because I was already stretched thin with the required stuff. If it wasn't going to affect my grade directly, it wasn't happening.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or exam, I'd do it. Activities that had clear, immediate payoff felt worth my time. Also, anything that didn't take forever—I didn't have the energy for things that dragged on. If other students said something was helpful, I'd give it a shot too.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With my resources being limited and having so much required work, optional activities were the first thing I cut. Required activities at least had consequences, so I'd push through even when I didn't want to. Optional ones just made me feel guilty for not doing them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download videos and watch them on my phone during commutes or at work breaks. I borrowed textbooks from friends instead of buying them online. A lot of my studying happened with handwritten notes I'd make from those downloaded materials. The LMS wouldn't know about any of that.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours working through problems on paper or practicing concepts by hand, but none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with classmates in person—we'd work through stuff together without logging into anything. My best learning moments were probably invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then use it repeatedly offline. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab things when I could and work with them later. The LMS probably thought I only looked at something once when really I went over it multiple times on my own.
Q12: There was a rough patch around midterms when everything felt pointless. I was sad a lot, dealing with personal stuff, and just couldn't bring myself to care about coursework. My attendance dropped even more during that time. Having the peer support helped a bit, but some days I just couldn't show up—physically or mentally.
Q13: Absolutely. Being tired from everything else in life made the LMS feel like another burden. When I was confused about course material, I'd avoid logging in because it stressed me out. Competing responsibilities always won over schoolwork. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later kept getting pushed back.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, not really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive. My mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, family, whatever was going on.
Q15: I put off discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. Writing my thoughts out for others to see made me anxious, so I'd delay until the last minute or skip them. Bigger assignments got postponed too because they felt overwhelming. I knew it was hurting me, but starting felt impossible sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd check the LMS obsessively as deadlines approached, cramming everything in at once. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I functioned. The panic of a deadline was more motivating than any interest in the material.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it helped me know what to focus on next time. But feedback often came too late to change anything for that assignment. Positive comments from peers in the support group motivated me more than formal feedback, honestly. Knowing someone noticed my effort mattered.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my chaotic life. Having so many modules and deadlines spread across 12 courses made it hard to keep track. I wished there was more flexibility for people like me who can't always be consistent. The structure assumed everyone had the same amount of time and energy.
Q19: Shorter, chunked content was easier to handle than long lectures. When things were clearly labeled as high-priority versus optional, that helped me focus my limited energy. But some courses buried important information in confusing navigation, which made me give up looking. Clear organization made a huge difference.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need without finding it, or they're anxious and keep checking things without actually working. Could also be people like me who are trying to engage but get distracted or overwhelmed before completing anything. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. My exam scores were decent even though I barely showed up online because I studied in ways the LMS couldn't track. I'm good at cramming and figuring things out from minimal information. Some people just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn the material.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you had them open, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline work, the mental effort, the times you're struggling but not asking for help. They see quantity of interaction, not quality of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real effort to understand it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's being mentally present. For me, engagement happened in bursts when I could connect material to something real. It's hard to stay engaged when you're drifting through life.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally while still trying to keep up. It wouldn't capture the peer conversations that kept me from dropping out, or the offline hours I spent teaching myself. It also wouldn't show that despite everything, I actually did learn things—I just learned them my own way, on my own terms.
